transcript:
Watch any single arc rather than the whole field, and you'll see each one starts as a crisp, cool-toned curve — the code literally calls this phase "the beauty of known destination," rendered in that clinical cyan-to-white gradient. Then, at a randomly earned moment — the variable is called release threshold — the line begins to warp off its calculated path. That drift isn't noise for noise's sake; it's built from coherent drift, the same kind of function you'd use to make smoke or wind look believable, so multiple arcs bend toward the same invisible pull.

Here's the detail I love: some lines don't get to release at all. About 30 percent are flagged as persists in knowing — they just... finish their original certain path, cyan and precise, no warmth, no wandering. And the ones that do loosen sometimes flicker back — "grasping again" moments, marked by those small perpendicular ticks — brief, almost embarrassed attempts to recalculate certainty before letting go once more.

So what you're watching isn't chaos happening to structure. It's structure *earning* its right to stop knowing where it's going.

cues.target: Before release: precise, cool, the beauty of known destination
